entry.title: AI Agents: What Happens When AI Starts Taking Action

link.title: AI Agents: What Happens When AI Starts Taking Action

entry.content:
A chatbot answers questions. An AI agent does something more interesting — it makes decisions, uses tools, and takes a sequence of steps to complete a goal.

If LLMs are the brain, agents are what happen when you give that brain hands.

What Makes Something an Agent?

A basic language model takes input and produces output. One turn, done.

An agent operates in a loop:

  1. It receives a goal.
  2. It decides what action to take.
  3. It takes that action — searching the web, writing code, calling an API, reading a file.
  4. It looks at the result.
  5. It decides what to do next.

This continues until the goal is complete — or the agent gets stuck.

A Concrete Example

Say you ask an AI agent: “Research the top three AI tools for recruiters and summarise them in a table.”

A chatbot would try to answer from memory, probably giving you outdated information.

An agent would:

  • Search the web for recent articles on AI recruitment tools
  • Open and read several pages
  • Compare the tools across features, pricing, and reviews
  • Write a summary table based on what it actually found

Same question, very different process.

The Tools Agents Use

Agents get access to tools — functions they can call to interact with the world. Common ones include:

  • Web search — find current information
  • Code execution — write and run code to solve problems
  • File reading/writing — work with documents
  • API calls — interact with external services like calendars, databases, or email

The agent decides which tool to use and when, based on the goal and what it’s learned so far.

Why This Is a Big Deal

Most real-world tasks aren’t single questions — they’re processes. Research, then analyse, then draft, then send. Agents can handle multi-step workflows that would otherwise require a human in the loop at every stage.

This is why agents are showing up in software engineering, customer support, and business operations — anywhere a task has multiple steps that currently need a human at every stage.

What to Watch Out For

Agents need guardrails. An agent that can take actions can also take the wrong ones — deleting files, sending premature emails, or looping on a task it can’t complete.

Good agent design includes human checkpoints, especially for high-stakes tasks. Autonomy and oversight work together, not against each other.

Francis Sitawa Ongoro is an AI & Fullstack Engineer based in Nairobi, Kenya.
entry.summary: A chatbot answers questions. An AI agent goes further — it makes decisions, uses tools, and takes steps to complete a goal. Here's how that works.

entry.title: What Is a Large Language Model and How Does It Actually Work?

link.title: What Is a Large Language Model and How Does It Actually Work?

entry.content:
ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude — you’ve heard the names. They’re all Large Language Models, or LLMs. But what does that actually mean? And how does a piece of software produce text that sounds like a human wrote it?

Let’s break it down without the jargon.

It Starts With Prediction

At its core, an LLM does one thing: it predicts the next word.

Given the sentence “The capital of France is…“, it predicts “Paris”. Given “She opened the door and…“, it predicts something that fits — “walked in”, “screamed”, whatever makes sense in context.

That sounds simple. But do this billions of times, across hundreds of billions of words of text, and something remarkable happens: the model starts to develop a deep sense of language, facts, reasoning patterns, even tone.

How It Learns

LLMs are trained on massive amounts of text — books, websites, code, articles. During training, the model reads a sentence, tries to predict the next word, checks if it got it right, and adjusts its internal settings slightly based on the error. Repeat this trillions of times.

The model never memorises text word for word. Instead, it learns patterns — grammar, facts, how arguments are structured, how questions are typically answered.

What “Large” Actually Means

The “large” in Large Language Model refers to the number of parameters — the internal numerical settings the model adjusts during training. GPT-4 is estimated to have over a trillion parameters. More parameters generally means more capacity to capture nuance and handle complex tasks.

More parameters also means more computing power needed, which is why training frontier models costs tens of millions of dollars.

Why They Sound So Human

Because they’re trained on human-written text. The model has seen how humans explain things, argue, joke, apologise, and teach. It learned to mirror those patterns.

It doesn’t understand in the way you do. There’s no meaning behind the words for the model — just statistical relationships between tokens. But those relationships are rich enough to produce output that’s often indistinguishable from human writing.

The Limitation to Keep in Mind

LLMs are frozen at training time. They don’t learn from your conversations, and they don’t know what happened after their training cutoff. That’s exactly why RAG (see the previous post) exists — to give them access to fresh, specific information at query time.

Francis Sitawa Ongoro is an AI & Fullstack Engineer based in Nairobi, Kenya.
entry.summary: ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude — they're all Large Language Models. But what does that actually mean, and how do they produce such convincing text?

entry.title: How AI Finds and Uses Information: A Beginner’s Guide to RAG

link.title: How AI Finds and Uses Information: A Beginner’s Guide to RAG

entry.content:
Ever asked an AI chatbot a question and gotten a confidently wrong answer? That’s not a random glitch — it’s a fundamental limitation of how most AI models work. And the fix has a name: RAG.

RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. The idea is simple once you see it.

The Problem: AI Works From Memory Alone

A standard AI language model is like a very well-read person who studied everything on the internet up to a certain date — then went into a room with no phone, no books, and no Wi-Fi.

They’ll answer from memory. But if something changed after they went in, they won’t know. And if they half-remember something, they’ll fill in the gaps with a guess — and sound completely confident doing it. Researchers call this hallucination.

The Fix: Give the AI a Way to Look Things Up

RAG adds a retrieval step before the AI responds:

  1. You ask a question.
  2. The system searches a knowledge base — documents, PDFs, records — and pulls the most relevant pieces.
  3. Those pieces get passed to the AI alongside your question.
  4. The AI answers using both what it knows and what it just retrieved.

The AI is no longer working from memory alone.

A Simple Analogy

You’re a customer support agent. A customer asks about the current savings interest rate. You don’t guess — you search the company handbook and give an accurate answer.

That’s RAG. The handbook is the knowledge base. The search is retrieval. Your response is generation.

Why It Matters

  • Stays current — update the knowledge base without retraining the model.
  • More accurate — the AI works from real documents, not reconstructed memory.
  • Transparent — you can show users exactly which source the answer came from.

This is why RAG powers legal research tools, medical assistants, enterprise knowledge bases, and customer support bots.

What RAG Doesn’t Fix

RAG is powerful but not magic. If the knowledge base has bad information, the AI will use it. If retrieval pulls the wrong documents, the answer suffers. Think of it as giving the AI a library card — whether it finds the right book still matters.

Francis Sitawa Ongoro is an AI & Fullstack Engineer based in Nairobi, Kenya.
entry.summary: Ever wondered why ChatGPT sometimes makes things up — and how smarter AI systems get around that? The answer is RAG.
